topics.dek: One connection expands what is real. The other expands what is imagined.
topics.answer: A Jupiter connection, where one person's card falls in the other's Jupiter position, carries generosity and mutual benefit: these bonds tend to enrich both people materially and spiritually. A Neptune connection carries dreams and idealization: inspiring at best, foggy at worst. The pair makes a useful study in contrast because one expands reality and the other expands imagination.

sections.heading: Jupiter: the benefactor current
body: The Jupiter person tends to bring blessing into your life: opportunities, generosity, genuine goodwill, and often tangible benefit. The tradition considers it one of the best positions for business partnership and marriage alike.
Its quiet risk is one-way flow. If the Jupiter current always runs the same direction, the receiver can drift into entitlement and the giver into fatigue. Healthy Jupiter bonds circulate.

sections.heading: Neptune: the dream current
body: The Neptune person appears to you slightly haloed: more ideal, more fascinating, more like the answer than any person actually is. Neptune connections inspire art, spiritual growth, and long devotion.

They also host the deck's best-documented disappointment pattern: the fog lifts, an ordinary human stands where the ideal stood, and the reckoning arrives. The connection was never false; it was partly imagined, which is different.

sections.heading: Telling them apart in practice
body: A rough field test: Jupiter bonds leave a track record of concrete good, things that happened and helped. Neptune bonds leave a track record of feelings and visions, which may be precious but weigh nothing.
Neither needs to be avoided. Jupiter asks you to keep the generosity mutual; Neptune asks you to enjoy the dream while budgeting for the human.

faqs.q: Is a Jupiter connection good for business?
faqs.a: It is the classic pairing for it: mutual benefit and expansion are the position's core themes. The usual diligence still applies; a good current is not a contract.

faqs.q: How do I know if I am idealizing a Neptune connection?
faqs.a: Ask what you know about the person versus what you feel about them. A large gap in favor of feeling is the Neptune signature.

faqs.q: Can one relationship carry both currents?
faqs.a: Yes. Positions run each direction separately, so one person can be your Jupiter while you are their Neptune, which makes for a generous but asymmetric fog.

topics.title: How to Compare Two Birth Cards
topics.description: A five-step order for comparing any two birth cards: suits, ranks, spread positions both directions, ruling cards, and the honest summary.
topics.dek: Any two cards can be compared in five steps, in order, without a score.
topics.answer: Compare two birth cards in a fixed order. First, suits: same domain or translation needed. Second, ranks: what movement each card wants. Third, spread positions in both directions, since the roles are rarely symmetrical. Fourth, ruling cards for tone. Fifth, a plain-language summary of where the pair flows and where it works. No step produces a score, and none should.

sections.heading: Steps one and two: the cards themselves
body: Suits first. Same suit means shared currency and shared blind spots; different suits mean translation work and built-in balance. Neither is better; they are different jobs.
Ranks second. Ask what movement each card wants: starting, stabilizing, changing, completing, ruling. Matched speeds feel easy; mismatched speeds are where the pace arguments come from.

sections.heading: Step three: positions, both directions
body: Look up where each card sits in the other's Life Spread. This is the step people skip and the one carrying the most information: the positions are usually different in each direction.
You may be their Venus while they are your Saturn. Asymmetries like that explain relationships where the two people describe the same bond in bafflingly different terms.

sections.heading: Steps four and five: tone, then honesty
body: Ruling cards add each person's expressive style. Two compatible birth cards with clashing ruling cards feel harder day-to-day than the core comparison suggests; the reverse is also true.

Finish with three plain sentences: where this pair flows, where it grinds, and what each person would need to say out loud for the grind to soften. If you cannot write those sentences, the comparison is not done.

faqs.q: Why not just use a compatibility score?
faqs.a: A score hides the structure that makes the reading useful. Two pairs with the same number can have completely different work to do.

faqs.q: Do I need both birthdays exactly?
faqs.a: Month and day for both people, yes. Birth cards cannot be guessed from personality, and a wrong card produces a confidently wrong comparison.

faqs.q: What if the comparison looks bad?
faqs.a: No comparison is a verdict. Hard positions name where the work is; plenty of long, good relationships live on structurally demanding pairings.

topics.title: Naming Repeating Relationship Patterns
topics.description: Using Cardology to see why the same relationship keeps happening with different people: the card behind the type, and the practice of naming it.
topics.dek: You do not keep meeting the same person. You keep running the same pattern.
topics.answer: When the same relationship dynamic keeps recurring with different people, Cardology offers a structural first question: is there a repeated card, suit, or spread position among the people involved? Often there is. Naming the recurring position, and your own card's role in attracting it, turns a fate-flavored complaint into a workable pattern.

sections.heading: Collect the data first
body: List the significant relationships where the pattern ran, and calculate each person's birth card. This takes ten minutes and replaces a mood with a dataset.
Then look for the repeat: the same card twice, the same suit throughout, or, most often, the same spread position: a run of people who all sat in your Saturn, or your Mars, or your Neptune.

sections.heading: Read your own card into it
body: The recurring position is half the story; your card's pattern is the other half. A card whose shadow gives too much will keep finding takers, and a card that avoids direct conflict will keep hiring people to have it anyway.
This is the least comfortable and most useful step. The people were different; the caster of the role was you, and the role is written in a range your own card describes.

links.label: How to Read a Birth Card Meaning

sections.heading: From named to changed
body: Naming is not fixing, but nothing gets fixed unnamed. Once the pattern has a card-language handle, you can catch it early: the familiar charge of a new person landing in the old position.
The choice at that moment is the actual work: run the pattern again with better awareness, or respond differently to it for once. The cards can only make the moment visible.

faqs.q: Is it really likely my exes share card patterns?
faqs.a: It is common enough to be worth checking, especially at the level of spread positions rather than identical cards. And if no pattern shows up, that is also information.

faqs.q: Does a repeating pattern mean something karmic?
faqs.a: The tradition would say the repetition is the lesson presenting itself again. You can drop the metaphysics and keep the practical core: repetition marks unfinished work.

faqs.q: Can I break a pattern the cards describe?
faqs.a: The cards describe defaults, not sentences. People change their responses to a pattern all the time; that is the entire point of seeing it.
